data.title: New Fortress Energy wins Puerto Rico approval for long-term gas supply
data.description:
New Fortress Energy (NFE) +17.1% post-market Thursday after saying it received final approval for a seven-year gas agreement from Puerto Rico's Financial Oversight and Management Board to supply liquefied natural gas to the island.

The contract, valued at an estimated $3.2 billion, will secure the delivery of ~75 TBtu of natural gas to help enhance grid stability and support cleaner power generation across Puerto Rico's energy system, New Fortress (NFE) said.

Earlier this week, the contract received conditional approval from the Puerto Rico regulator after an intense review.

Two weeks ago, the company warned it may seek U.S. bankruptcy protection if it fails in its efforts to negotiate an out-of-court restructuring with lenders.

data.title: ChargePoint outlines $100M–$110M Q4 revenue target as debt reduction and new products drive growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. (CHPT) Q3 2026

Management View

  • Richard Wilmer, President, CEO & Director, stated that "Financial performance this quarter exceeded expectations. Revenue surpassed the top end of our guidance, reaching $106 million, which marks a return to growth." He emphasized ongoing momentum with new products, citing the Eaton partnership and European expansion as key growth drivers for the second half of calendar 2026. Wilmer highlighted, "Our non-GAAP gross margin remained at a record high of 33%. We maintained strict cash discipline with cash utilization better than planned at $14 million." He also noted a completed debt exchange, securing nearly $110 million of deal discounts, reducing outstanding debt by $172 million and extending maturity to 2030. Wilmer described this as "a pivotal step in strengthening our financial foundation."
  • Wilmer detailed product and market developments, including the extension of a partnership with the City of New York and a new program with BMW North America for destination charging stations. He asserted, "ChargePoint now manages approximately 375,000 ports including more than 39,000 DC fast chargers and more than 127,000 ports located in Europe."
  • Wilmer commented on innovation: "Our new DC product line ChargePoint Express powered by Eaton is a bidirectional capable solution that we believe can be deployed with up to 30% lower capital expenditure, occupies a 30% smaller footprint and reduces ongoing operational costs by up to 30% compared to other solutions."
  • Mansi Khetani, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer, stated, "Revenue for the third quarter exceeded our expectations coming in at $106 million, significantly above the high end of our guidance range of $90 million to $100 million up 7% sequentially and up 6% year-on-year." She highlighted that "Non-GAAP gross margin remained at a record high of 33%, flat sequentially and up 7 percentage points year-on-year." Khetani added, "Non-GAAP operating expenses were $57 million, representing a 2% reduction, both sequentially and year-on-year."

Outlook

  • Khetani provided guidance: "For the fourth quarter of fiscal 2026, we expect revenue to be $100 million to $110 million representing a 3% year-on-year growth at the midpoint." She stated, "While we remain cautious in light of the broader macroeconomic environment, we are confident that revenue growth will continue as we execute on our strategic priorities."
  • Management indicated ongoing focus on new product ramp, European expansion, and the Eaton partnership as growth catalysts for the second half of 2026.

Financial Results

  • Khetani reported, "Subscription revenue at $42 million was 40% of total revenue up 5% sequentially and up 15% year-on-year as our total installed base continues to grow. Other revenue at $7 million was 7% of total revenue."
  • Non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA loss was $19 million, improving from $22 million in the prior quarter and $29 million in the third quarter of last year. Stock-based compensation was $15 million, down from $18 million last quarter.
  • Inventory balance was stable at $212 million. Cash balance was $181 million compared to $195 million in the prior quarter, reflecting cash usage of $14 million.
  • Khetani described the debt exchange: "We completed a privately negotiated debt exchange with existing holders that will ultimately reduce our total debt by $172 million, more than half of the previous balance... and extension of debt maturity from 2028 to 2030."

Q&A

  • Colin Rusch, Oppenheimer: Asked about product evolution and demand from virtual power plants. Wilmer responded, "The new Flex product line... will start rolling out in 2026... our new Express line... can integrate directly with the DC grid... provides not only full bidirectional charging but very significant economic benefits in terms of CapEx and OpEx."
  • Rusch: Follow-up on inventory reduction. Khetani answered, "We will see a small decline in Q4, most likely in the inventory balance but we expect a more material decrease throughout next fiscal year as we sell through the existing inventory."
  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Inquired on gross margin potential with new products. Khetani said, "Improvements in hardware margin in the near term will be entirely driven by product mix... we expect a larger improvement from Asia manufacturing as we sell through our existing inventory... that should come in towards the latter half of next year."
  • Christopher Pierce, Needham: Asked about confidence in European projects and timing. Wilmer explained, "I was in Europe recently... the response was overwhelmingly positive... I'm quite confident that we'll win a number of fairly significant deals in Europe as we bring that product to market in the second half of next year."
  • William Peterson, JPMorgan: Queried about revenue beat and profitability. Khetani noted, "The significant beat was mostly due to a boost in residential billings due to the expiration of the federal EV credits... we're not guiding to a time frame, but EBITDA... will come with growth in revenue which we are significantly focused on."
  • Craig Irwin, ROTH Capital: Asked about NEVI funding and state support. Wilmer replied, "With respect to NEVI, we are seeing projects move forward... 40 states now are active in NEVI and awarding contracts, and we're active in many of those."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions focused on inventory management, margin improvements, European growth, and the sustainability of recent revenue gains, reflecting a cautiously optimistic but probing tone.
  • Management responded with a confident and constructive tone, emphasizing product innovation, partnerships, margin resiliency, and prudent expense control. Phrases such as "we are confident that revenue growth will continue" and "our solutions do more than meet expectations they are redefining them" underscored this.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited a more positive tone, with management showing increased confidence and analysts concentrating on tangible growth catalysts and risk mitigation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue grew from $99 million in Q2 to $106 million in Q3, with non-GAAP gross margin steady at 33%.
  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism to a firmer expectation of growth, supported by debt reduction and new product launches.
  • Analysts' focus moved from concerns about macroeconomic headwinds and delays to concrete questions about inventory reduction, margin gains, and European expansion.
  • Management’s confidence increased, reflected in detailed commentary on innovation, financial discipline, and partnership execution. Analyst sentiment became less skeptical, focusing more on operational execution and upside from new initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty and the importance of prudent expense management.
  • Khetani addressed inventory risk, noting strategic decisions to wind down certain manufacturer commitments and expectation of gradual inventory reduction.
  • Wilmer mentioned industry consolidation as a trend, which could present both risks and opportunities for ChargePoint’s market position.

Final Takeaway

ChargePoint's third quarter marked a return to growth, driven by new product launches, an expanded partnership with Eaton, and accelerating opportunities in Europe. The company reported record gross margins, disciplined cash management, and a transformative debt reduction that strengthens its balance sheet and positions it for future growth. Management is confident in its ability to deliver innovation, capture market share, and improve margins, with clear expectations for continued revenue gains into the next quarter and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Smith & Wesson projects Q3 sales growth of 8%–10% amid inventory reduction and robust new product momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Smith & Wesson Brands, Inc. (SWBI) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Mark Smith, President, CEO & Director, stated that "excellent efficiency in our business operations allowed us to deliver solid profitability of $15 million of EBITDAS on net sales of nearly $125 million." He highlighted a "significant reduction in inventory," generating "healthy operating cash flow of over $27 million in the quarter." New products accounted for "nearly 40% of sales in the quarter." Smith emphasized that "handgun outperformance far outweighed the impact of long gun seasonality," with overall average selling prices (ASPs) rising 3.5% year-over-year and sequentially up 6.5%.
  • Smith reported that "channel inventory at distributors continues to be very clean declining over 5% sequentially and over 15% year-on-year, positioning us to quickly convert incremental demand into shipments as we move into our typically busy second half of the fiscal year."
  • Smith provided an update on the new Smith & Wesson Academy, stating, "just over 2 months in, we have already had the pleasure of hosting dozens of current and prospective law enforcement customers and held our first consumer training classes. The feedback has been overwhelmingly positive."
  • Deana McPherson, Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Assistant Secretary, noted, "Net sales for our second quarter of $124.7 million were $5 million or 3.9% below the prior year." She reported that "gross margin of 24.3% was down 2.3% versus a year ago, due primarily to decreased absorption on temporarily lower production as we focus on inventory optimization and an 80 basis point negative impact from tariffs." McPherson added, "the lower revenue and associated margin resulted in net income of $1.9 million compared with $4.5 million in the prior year period. Earnings per share during the second quarter was $0.04 compared with $0.10 a year ago."

Outlook

  • McPherson stated, "we expect our third quarter sales will be 8% to 10% over our Q3 fiscal 2025 sales with no significant impact either positively or negatively from channel inventory."
  • She explained that "Q3 gross margins to increase by a few percentage points, both sequentially and year-over-year. Operating expenses in Q3 will likely be about 15% higher than in Q2 with increases due to the SHOT Show in January, new product development costs, increased promotions and increased profit sharing." The company also expects "continued healthy cash generation through the second half of the fiscal year. Our effective tax rate is expected to be approximately 28%."

Financial Results

  • McPherson reported that "hand gun ASPs increased slightly from Q1 levels due to strong demand for certain premium products, partially offset by promotions and continued demand for lower-priced products. Long gun ASPs increased due to the mix of higher-priced products and slightly increased overall volume."
  • Operating expenses were $26.2 million, which was $733,000 lower than a year ago, with the increase in selling and marketing costs for the Academy grand opening offset by lower G&A primarily due to lower legal costs.
  • Cash generated from operations was $27.3 million compared with cash used from operations of $7.4 million in the prior year quarter due primarily to lower inventory and income taxes paid. Inventory decreased $20 million versus an increase of $6.2 million in the prior year quarter.
  • The company spent $11 million on capital projects, paid $5.8 million in dividends, ended the quarter with $27.3 million in cash and investments, and had $90 million in borrowings on its line of credit. Since quarter end, $15 million has been repaid, bringing borrowings to $75 million.

Q&A

  • Alex Sturnieks, Lake Street Capital Markets: "You noted an 80-point or 80 basis point headwind in the quarter. But could you just walk us through what you're seeing in input costs right now, steel components, tariffs and how you're thinking about gross margins over the next couple of quarters?" Mark Smith answered that "our impact from tariffs, you can probably expect it to pick up a little bit as we go through the back half of the year just as we work through some of the inventory that we had already in stock from kind of the pre-tariff days. But it shouldn't have a material impact on our profitability as we go through the back half." Deana McPherson added, "given that inventory has declined and we're now ramping back up, the absorption will probably be a little bit favorable. So you'll see a little bit of a positive impact that should be able to offset that impact of tariff costs."
  • Sturnieks: "OpEx looked really clean this quarter, specifically G&A. Is this a level you feel you can hold on to? Or should we expect G&A to tick up as we move through the rest of the year?" Mark Smith replied, "our operating expenses are usually fairly consistent year-to-year. So we always have an increase for SHOT Show in January... We're pretty disciplined in managing the OpEx line in general."
  • Sturnieks: "It sounds like you're seeing some nice tailwinds given the Q3 outlook. Any early thoughts on how Q4 is shaping up from where you sit today?" Mark Smith responded, "Q4 always is our strongest quarter. And this year, I don't think it's going to be any different. I think you can expect somewhere high single-digit, low double-digit growth in Q4 over Q3 this year."
  • Rommel Dionisio, Aegis Capital: "I wonder if you could just give us a little heads up in terms of the feedback you're receiving with regards to reset for new products, outlook for calendar 2026 in the industry overall?" Mark Smith answered, "the conversations we've been on with our -- whether it's distributors, retailers or all of our channel partners have been very positive around Smith & Wesson... Their inventory is very clean across the line and performing efficiently for them."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on cost headwinds, operating expense discipline, and market outlook, with a generally neutral but inquisitive tone, seeking clarification on input costs and margin visibility.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing efficiency, inventory discipline, and product momentum. Phrases such as "we believe we are well positioned for continued success" and "the strength of the brand is really starting to show through" reflected ongoing confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management sentiment remained steady, with management slightly more upbeat on inventory progress and product acceptance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q3 shifted from a general expectation of "normal seasonal environment" and sales growth over Q1 (in Q1 call) to a specific target of "8% to 10% over our Q3 fiscal 2025 sales."
  • Management's tone was more confident regarding inventory management, noting progress in reducing inventory and positioning for the seasonally strong second half.
  • Analyst focus shifted from product pricing strategies and legacy product inventory (Q1) to input cost pressures, margin outlook, and operational expense discipline (Q2).
  • Key metrics such as net sales, gross margin, and EPS were lower than the prior year quarter but marked a sequential improvement from Q1.
  • Strategic priorities remained constant, with a continued emphasis on new product innovation, channel inventory management, and capital discipline.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the ongoing risk of tariffs impacting gross margins, though they stated, "it shouldn't have a material impact on our profitability as we go through the back half."
  • Macroeconomic uncertainty was noted as a factor that could affect performance in the coming quarters.
  • Analysts probed for possible increases in input costs and the sustainability of current operating expense levels.
  • Management's mitigation strategies include disciplined cost management, leveraging operational efficiency, and maintaining flexibility in promotional activity.

Final Takeaway

Smith & Wesson Brands, Inc. reported strong operational discipline in Q2 2026, highlighted by significant inventory reduction, robust new product momentum, and clean channel inventory positioning. Management projects third quarter sales growth of 8% to 10% over the prior year, supported by expectations of higher gross margins and continued healthy cash generation. The company remains focused on innovation, operational efficiency, and capital discipline to drive future growth, while monitoring macroeconomic and tariff-related risks as it enters its seasonally strongest period.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Humana, Mark Cuban potentially teaming up for pharmacy partnership
data.description:
• Humana (HUM) and billionaire businessman Mark Cuban, one of the founders of discount online pharmacy Cost Plus Drugs, are working on a potential partnership.
  • Speaking at the Forbes Healthcare Summit in New York Thursday, Cuban and Humana CEO Jim Rechtin said that the arrangement would simplify the drug supply chain.
  • Cuban said that the discussions have dealt with "putting together direct-to-employer programs," Bloomberg reported.
  • Cost Plus Drugs aims to undermine pharmacy benefit managers, the entities that traditionally run prescription drug programs for insurers, by eliminating rebates and spread pricing, providing drugs at a savings to patients directly.
  • President Trump has been a vocal opponent of PBMs, who are colloquially known as "drug middlemen." The PBM industry, in response to criticism, has reportedly been working on reforms to assuage regulators.

data.title: Gold gains, silver slips as investors assess U.S. jobs data
data.description:
Gold futures edged higher while silver slipped Thursday, as the latest data on the U.S. workforce likely did not move the needle on the Federal Reserve's monetary policy trajectory, with the FOMC widely expected to reduce interest rates by a quarter-point next week.

Data on Thursday showed new U.S. unemployment benefit claims declined last week to 191,000, the lowest level in more than three years and well below expectations of 220,000 according to economists polled by Reuters, while Wednesday's ADP report showed U.S. private payrolls fell by 32,000 in November, marking the steepest drop in more than two and a half years.

The data suggests employers are still largely holding onto workers despite recent layoff announcements.

Investors are awaiting September's Personal Consumption Expenditures report, the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, due on Friday.

"Markets aren't going to do very much between now and next week, and as far as gold is concerned, probably we will be stuck in a relatively uneventful trading range for a while," Marex analyst Edward Meir said in a note, adding gold will not retest its previous peak of nearly $4,400/oz this year.

"The data reinforced expectations that interest rates will come down in December," ADM Investor Services wrote. "Markets are also expecting a dovish tilt from the Fed in the longer term as Kevin Hassett is expected to become the next head of the Fed when Powell's term is over."

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for December delivery closed +0.3% to $4,211.80/oz, the yellow metal's eighth gain in nine sessions, but front-month Comex December silver (XAGUSD:CUR) ended -1.8% to $56.847/oz, its third consecutive daily decline.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Vanda jumps as FDA lifts tradipitant partial clinical hold
data.description:
• Vanda Pharmaceuticals (VNDA) has surged ~7% in after-hours trading Thursday after the US FDA lifted its partial clinical hold on tradipitant for motion sickness.
  • The drugmaker said that the FDA agreed that "motion sickness is an acute, self-limiting physiologic response rather than a chronic or chronic-intermittent condition. The Agency therefore concluded that the use of tradipitant in motion sickness represents an acute, event-driven therapy, eliminating the need for an additional six-month dog toxicity study and rendering the partial clinical hold unnecessary."
  • The hold was initially placed due to safety concerns and a lack of chronic toxicity data. 
  • Separately, the FDA is set to issue a decision on an NDA for tradipitant for prevention of vomiting caused by nausea by Dec. 30.

data.title: MDA Space announces offering of C$250M senior unsecured notes due 2030
data.description:
• MDA Space (MDA:CA) on Thursday said it has priced a private placement offering and entered into an underwriting agreement to sell, pursuant to the offering, C$250 million aggregate principal amount of 7.00% senior unsecured notes due 2030.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about December 23, 2025.
  • The notes will be issued at a price of C$1,000 per C$1,000 principal amount of notes. 
  • The company intends to use the net proceeds of the offering to refinance existing outstanding indebtedness under the Credit Agreement.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Mercado Libre issues $750M of 2033 senior unsecured notes
data.description:
• Mercado Libre (MELI) on Thursday announced the issuance of its 2033 senior unsecured notes for a total amount of $750 million.
  • Transaction Highlights
    • Title of Securities: 4.900% Notes due 2033
    • Security Type: Senior Unsecured Notes
    • Format: SEC Registered
    • Issuer Rating: BBB- (S&P) / BBB- (Fitch)
    • Size: USD 750 million
    • Tenor: 7 years (expiring in January 2033)
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: CDC vaccine panel voting on hepatitis b shots postponed over confusion
data.description:
An influential vaccine advisory panel to the CDC delayed voting on questions regarding if and when to administer the hepatitis b shot to newborns amid confusion over the language of the questions themselves.

Although the voting questions were posted on Wednesday, some Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices members complained that the language of the voting questions had been changed several times.

"This is the third version of the questions that most of the ACIP members received in 72 hours," said Joseph Hibbeln, the former acting chief of the Section of Nutritional Neurosciences at the National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism and a psychiatrist. "We don’t even have a slide explicitly describing what questions we're supposed to answer."

ACIP members ended up voting 7-3 to delay voting until Friday.

The first question, as published by ACIP on Wednesday, stated that the offspring of mothers who test negative for the virus should discuss with their health care provider about whether their child should get the vaccine, a process known as shared decision-making. Mothers who decide to skip the birth dose should wait at least two months should they decide to have their child receive the series of shots later on. 

The question also recommends that children of mothers who test positive for hepatitis b get the vaccine at birth.

A second question stated that in cases where a mother's hepatitis b status is unknown, the child should receive the shot. An earlier version had stated that the decision in these cases should be made in consultation with a doctor. 

The third question asks whether serology testing should be offered  -- and covered by insurance -- before subsequent vaccine dose administration. The hepatitis b vaccine is usually given across three doses by the time a child is 18 months old.  

The delay is the second for the vaccine after voting was tabled at the group's previous meeting in September. 

The three hepatitis b vaccines approved in the US are: Merck's (MRK) Recombivax HB; GSK's (GSK) Engerix-B; and Dynavax Technologies' (DVAX) Heplisav-B.
